topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun glinted off the freshly sharpened edge of the aluminum stakes. From my window, I watched from behind the drawn curtains, my knuckles white where I gripped the windowsill. Mr. Henderson’s voice, booming across the lawn, was cheerful, entirely oblivious to the tension radiating from my small space. I could almost feel the sharp edges of his ambition pressing against my peace. Every time he mentioned the new perimeter, a tightness constricted my chest, making each breath feel shallow. I kept my gaze fixed on the intricate pattern of the wallpaper, a deliberate effort to keep my expression neutral, though a tremor ran through my hands as I adjusted the heavy drape.

[story 2]

"Just a little bit of wood, dear," Mrs. Gable had said, her tone light as a summer breeze, as she pointed towards the shared boundary. I stood on my porch, pretending to water the drooping petunias, but my feet felt rooted to the paving stones. The sight of the lumber truck idling down the street sent a cold wave washing over me. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles vibrating beneath my skin. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes, and mumbled something about the weather. Inside, the quiet hum of my own anxiety was deafening, a low, persistent thrumming that made my stomach churn with a dull ache.

[story 3]

I found myself pacing the length of my living room floor, the silence amplifying the frantic rhythm of my pulse. The sound of Mr. Henderson’s tools—the scrape of metal against wood—drifted through the thin walls, each sound an unwelcome intrusion. I stared at the framed photograph on the mantelpiece, the smiling faces seeming distant and unreachable. When he stopped to consult his plans, I instinctively pressed my palms against my eyes, a futile attempt to shield myself from the visual confirmation of his presence and his intent. My throat felt thick, constricted, as if I were swallowing sand, and I retreated to the darkest corner of the room, wishing the floorboards would simply swallow me whole.
